addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review

1	![ALT](./media/images/gemm-hierarchy-with-epilogue-no-labels.png "Complete CUDA GEMM decomposition")
2	# Overview
3	
4	# CUTLASS 4.6.0
5	
6	_CUTLASS 4.6.0 - June 2026_
7	
8	CUTLASS is a collection of abstractions for implementing high-performance matrix-matrix multiplication (GEMM)
9	and related computations at all levels and scales within CUDA. It incorporates strategies for
10	hierarchical decomposition and data movement. CUTLASS decomposes these "moving parts" into reusable, modular
11	software components and abstractions.
12	
13	Primitives for different levels of a conceptual parallelization hierarchy can be specialized and tuned
14	via custom tiling sizes, data types, and other algorithmic policy. The resulting flexibility simplifies
15	their use as building blocks within custom kernels and applications.
16	
17	CUTLASS has been providing CUDA C++ template abstractions for high-performance linear algebra since 2017 and
18	these abstractions provide extensive support for a wide range of computations including
19	mixed-precision computations, specialized data-movement (async copy) and
20	multiply-accumulate abstractions for FP64, FP32, TF32, FP16, BF16,
21	[FP32 emulation via tensor core instruction](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/tree/main/examples/27_ampere_3xtf32_fast_accurate_tensorop_gemm),
22	 8b floating point types (e5m2 and e4m3),
23	 block scaled data types (NVIDIA NVFP4 and OCP standard MXFP4, MXFP6, MXFP8),
24	 narrow integer types (4 and 8b signed and unsigned integers),
25	 and binary 1b data types (where architectures allow for the
26	native support of such data types) across NVIDIA's Volta, Turing, Ampere, Ada, Hopper, and Blackwell architectures.
27	
28	To this rich ecosystem of C++ based kernel programming abstractions, CUTLASS 4 adds CUTLASS DSLs. These are Python native interfaces for writing high-performance CUDA kernels based on core CUTLASS and CuTe concepts without any performance compromises. This allows for a much smoother learning curve, orders of magnitude faster compile times, native integration with DL frameworks without writing glue code, and much more intuitive metaprogramming that does not require deep C++ expertise.
29	
30	Overall we envision CUTLASS DSLs as a family of domain-specific languages (DSLs). With the release of 4.0, we are releasing the first of these in CuTe DSL. This is a low level programming model that is fully consistent with CuTe C++ abstractions — exposing core concepts such as layouts, tensors, hardware atoms, and full control over the hardware thread and data hierarchy.
31	
32	CuTe DSL demonstrates optimal matrix multiply and other linear algebra operations
33	targeting the programmable, high-throughput _Tensor Cores_ implemented by
34	NVIDIA's Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell architectures.
35	
36	We believe it will become an indispensable tool for students, researchers, and performance
37	engineers alike — flattening the learning curve of GPU programming, rapidly prototyping kernel
38	designs, and bringing optimized solutions into production.
39	
40	CuTe DSL is currently in public beta and will graduate out of beta by end of summer 2026.
41	
42	To get started quickly - please refer :
43	  - [CUTLASS C++ Quick Start Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/quickstart.html).
44	  - [CuTe DSL Quick Start Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/pythonDSL/quick_start.html).
45	
46	# What's New in CUTLASS 4.6
47	
48	## CuTe DSL
49	* New features
50	  - Supported AoT cross-compilation for aarch64?~@~Qlinux?~@~Qgnu
51	  - Support for two launch attributes: launch completion events (cudaLaunchAttributeLaunchCompletionEvent), for recording an event once all thread blocks have begun executing, and launch programatic events (cudaLaunchAttributeProgrammaticEvent), for PDL event-based synchronization
52	  - Supported auto calculating per-kernel shared memory carveout preference, or use new laucnch option `preferred_smem_carveout` to set manually.
53	  - Auto-deduced smem size for launching kernels
54	    - Launch config `smem` now defaults to `None` for auto-calculating kernel shared memory usage, which is recommended unless manual control is required.
55	    - Warnings will be raised when the manually set shared memory size is insufficient or exceeds the GPU maximum.
56	    - The default shared memory usage calculation aligns with CUDA C++ static shared memory behavior, i.e. summing all allocations additively.
57	    - An additional launch option `smem_merge_branch_allocs` is provided to merge shared memory allocations across mutually exclusive code branches, which is recommended for inlined mega-kernels to reduce total footprint.
58	
59	* Bug fixing and improvements
60	  - Improvements on linter support with more type ignores cleaned up
61	  - Improvements on tvm-ffi CUDA runtime error diagnostics
62	  - Improvements on dataclass support for TVM-FFI
63	  - Fixed a regression on compilation time
64	  - Enhancement on compile time checks to reject mis-aligned smem operand for TMA
65	  - Long-deprecated API clean-up, including:
66	    - cute.core.ThrMma, please use cute.ThrMma instead
67	    - cute.core.ThrCopy, please use cute.ThrCopy instead
68	    - cute.make_fragment, please use cute.make_rmem_tensor instead
69	
70	## CUTLASS C++
71	* Add [example 113](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/tree/main/examples/113_hopper_gemm_activation_fusion) for Hopper GEMM with activation fusion.
72	  - Supports standard and gated activations (e.g., SiLu) with fp8 and fp16 inputs.
73	  - Covers both regular GEMM and grouped GEMM variants.
74	* Improve SM90 grouped/ptr-array GEMM with EVT support.
75	  - Adds the EVT (Epilogue Visitor Tree) plumbing required to do activation, bias, and auxiliary-tensor fusion inside SM90 grouped and ptr-array GEMM kernels.
76	* Fix `DescriptorIterator::operator+` in `mma_traits_sm100.hpp` to use 32-bit arithmetic on CUDA toolkit version <= 13.3, preserving the high half of the smem descriptor.
77	
78	Note: CUTLASS 4.x builds are known to be down on Windows platforms for all CUDA toolkits.
79	CUTLASS team is working on a fix.
80	
81	**See the [CHANGELOG](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/CHANGELOG.html) for details of all past releases and updates.**
82	
83	# Performance
84	
85	CUTLASS primitives are very efficient.  When used to construct device-wide GEMM kernels,
86	they exhibit nearly optimal utilization of peak theoretical throughput. The figure below
87	shows CUTLASS 3.8's performance as a % of theoretical peak utilization
88	on various input and output data types when run on NVIDIA Blackwell SM100 architecture GPU.
89	
90	![ALT](media/images/cutlass-3.8-blackwell-gemm-peak-performance.svg "")
91	
92	The two figures below show the continual CUTLASS performance improvements
93	on an [NVIDIA H100](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/h100/) (NVIDIA Hopper architecture) since
94	CUTLASS 3.1.
95	CUTLASS 3.5.1 was compiled with the [CUDA 12.5u1 Toolkit](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads).
96	Tensor Core operations are implemented using CUDA's
97	[mma](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/index.html#warp-level-matrix-instructions-mma) and
98	[wgmma](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/index.html#asynchronous-warpgroup-level-matrix-instructions) instructions.
99	
100	![ALT](media/images/cutlass-3.5.1-gemm-peak-performance.png "")
101	![ALT](media/images/cutlass-3.5.1-gemm-peak-performance-fp8.png "")
102	
103	# CuTe
104	
105	CUTLASS 3.0 introduced a new core library, CuTe, to describe and manipulate tensors of threads and data.
106	CuTe is a collection of C++ CUDA template abstractions for
107	defining and operating on hierarchically multidimensional layouts of threads and data.
108	CuTe provides `Layout` and `Tensor` objects that compactly package the type,
109	shape, memory space, and layout of data, while performing the complicated indexing for the user.
110	This lets programmers focus on the logical descriptions of their algorithms while
111	CuTe does the mechanical bookkeeping for them. With these tools, we can quickly design,
112	implement, and modify all dense linear algebra operations.
113	
114	The core abstractions of CuTe are hierarchically multidimensional layouts
115	which can be composed with data arrays to represent tensors.
116	The representation of layouts is powerful enough to represent nearly
117	everything we need to implement efficient dense linear algebra.
118	Layouts can also be combined and manipulated via functional composition, on which we build a large set of common operations such as tiling and partitioning.
119	
120	CUTLASS 3.0 and beyond adopts CuTe throughout the GEMM hierarchy in its templates.
121	This greatly simplifies the design and improves code composability and readability.
122	More documentation specific to CuTe can be found in its
123	[dedicated documentation directory](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/cute/00_quickstart.html).
124	
125	# Compatibility
126	
127	Minimum requirements:
128	
129	- Architecture: Volta (compute capability 7.0)
130	- Compiler: Must support at least C++17
131	- CUDA Toolkit version: 11.4
132	
133	CUTLASS requires a C++17 host compiler and
134	performs best when built with the [**CUDA 12.8 Toolkit**](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads).
135	It is also compatible with CUDA 11.4, CUDA 11.5, CUDA 11.6, CUDA 11.7, CUDA 11.8, and all other CUDA 12.x versions.
136	
137	## Operating Systems
138	
139	We have tested the following environments.
140	
141	|**Operating System** | **Compiler** |
142	|-----------------|----------|
143	| Ubuntu 18.04 | GCC 7.5.0  |
144	| Ubuntu 20.04 | GCC 10.3.0 |
145	| Ubuntu 22.04 | GCC 11.2.0 |
146	
147	Note: GCC 8.5.0 has known regressions regarding fold expressions and overloaded operators. Using GCC 7.5.0 or (preferred) GCC >= 9 is recommended.
148	
149	Note: CUTLASS 3.x builds are known to be down on Windows platforms for all CUDA toolkits.
150	CUTLASS team is working on a fix.
151	
152	## Hardware
153	
154	CUTLASS runs successfully on the following NVIDIA GPUs, and it is expected to be efficient on Volta, Turing, Ampere, Ada, and Hopper architecture based NVIDIA GPUs.
155	
156	|**GPU**|**CUDA Compute Capability**|**Minimum CUDA Toolkit Required by CUTLASS-3**|
157	|---|---|---|
158	|NVIDIA V100 Tensor Core GPU            |7.0|11.4|
159	|NVIDIA TitanV                          |7.0|11.4|
160	|NVIDIA GeForce RTX 20x0 series         |7.5|11.4|
161	|NVIDIA T4                              |7.5|11.4|
162	|NVIDIA A100 Tensor Core GPU            |8.0|11.4|
163	|NVIDIA A10                             |8.6|11.4|
164	|NVIDIA GeForce RTX 30x0 series         |8.6|11.4|
165	|NVIDIA GeForce RTX 40x0 series         |8.9|11.8|
166	|NVIDIA L40                             |8.9|11.8|
167	|NVIDIA H100 Tensor Core GPU            |9.0|11.8|
168	|NVIDIA H200 Tensor Core GPU            |9.0|11.8|
169	|NVIDIA B200 Tensor Core GPU            |10.0|12.8|
170	|NVIDIA B300 Tensor Core GPU            |10.3|13.0|
171	|NVIDIA DRIVE Thor                      |11.0|13.0|
172	|NVIDIA GeForce RTX 50x0 series         |12.0|12.8|
173	|NVIDIA DGX Spark                       |12.1|13.0|
174	
175	## Target Architecture
176	
177	In general, PTX code generated for one target architecture can be run on future architectures
178	(i.e., it is forward compatible).
179	However, CUDA 12.0 introduced the concept of "architecture-accelerated features" whose
180	PTX does not have forward compatibility guarantees.
181	Several Hopper and Blackwell PTX instructions fall under this category of
182	architecture-accelerated features, and thus require a `sm_90a` or `sm100a` target architecture
183	(note the "a" appended). For more details on this and other architecture-accelerated instructions,
184	please refer to the [CUDA Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-c-programming-guide/index.html#feature-availability).
185	
186	The target architecture information is passed on to CUTLASS via the cmake flag
187	`CUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS`. In order to maximize performance on Hopper GH100,
188	users are required to build CUTLASS with `90a` as the target architecture.
189	If a user accidentally builds a kernel which uses SM90a features
190	(e.g. Hopper Tensor Core Instructions), using the SM90 target
191	(note the lack of "a"), with either CUDA Toolkit 12 or 11.8,
192	the kernel is expected to fail with a runtime error.
193	
194	```
195	cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS="90a"
196	```
197	Or
198	
199	```
200	cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS="100a"
201	```
202	
203	Note: The NVIDIA Blackwell SM100 architecture used in the datacenter
204	products has a different compute capability than the one underpinning
205	NVIDIA Blackwell GeForce RTX 50 series GPUs (SM120). As a result, kernels
206	compiled for Blackwell SM100 architecture with arch conditional features
207	(using `sm100a`) are not compatible with RTX 50 series GPUs.
208	
209	Please refer to the [functionality documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/functionality.html)
210	for details on which kernels require which target architectures.
211	
212	# Documentation
213	
214	CUTLASS is described in the following documents and the accompanying
215	[Doxygen documentation](https://nvidia.github.io/cutlass).
216	
217	- [Quick Start Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/quickstart.html) - basics of building and running CUTLASS
218	- [Functionality](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/functionality.html) - summarizes functionality available in CUTLASS
219	- [Efficient GEMM in CUDA](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/efficient_gemm.html) - describes how GEMM kernels may be implemented efficiently in CUDA
220	- [CUTLASS 3.x Design](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/cutlass_3x_design.html) - describes the CUTLASS 3.x design, its benefits, and how CuTe enables us to write much more composable components
221	- [GEMM API 3.x](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/gemm_api_3x.html) - describes the CUTLASS 3.x GEMM model and C++ template concepts
222	- [GEMM API 2.x](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/gemm_api.html) - describes the CUTLASS 2.x GEMM model and C++ template concepts
223	- [Implicit GEMM Convolution](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/implicit_gemm_convolution.html) - describes 2-D and 3-D convolution in CUTLASS
224	- [Code Organization](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/code_organization.html) - describes the organization and contents of the CUTLASS project
225	- [Terminology](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/terminology.html) - describes terms used in the code
226	- [Programming Guidelines](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/programming_guidelines.html) - guidelines for writing efficient modern CUDA C++
227	- [Fundamental types](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/fundamental_types.html) - describes basic C++ classes used in CUTLASS to represent numeric quantities and arrays
228	- [Layouts](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/layout.html) - describes layouts of matrices and tensors in memory
229	- [Tile Iterators](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/tile_iterator_concept.html) - describes C++ concepts for iterating over tiles of matrices in memory
230	- [CUTLASS Profiler](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/profiler.html) - command-line driven profiling application
231	- [CUTLASS Utilities](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/utilities.html) - additional templates used to facilitate rapid development
232	- [Dependent kernel launch](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/dependent_kernel_launch.html) - describes a new feature in Hopper which allows overlapping dependent
233	kernels in the same stream, and how it is used in CUTLASS.
234	
235	# Resources
236	We have also described the structure of an efficient GEMM in our talk at the
237	[GPU Technology Conference 2018](http://on-demand.gputechconf.com/gtc/2018/presentation/s8854-cutlass-software-primitives-for-dense-linear-algebra-at-all-levels-and-scales-within-cuda.pdf).
238	
239	- [CUTLASS: Software Primitives for Dense Linear Algebra at All Levels and Scales within CUDA](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/on-demand/session/gtcsiliconvalley2018-s8854/)
240	- [Developing CUDA Kernels to Push Tensor Cores to the Absolute Limit on NVIDIA A100](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/on-demand/session/gtcsj20-s21745/)
241	- [Accelerating Convolution with Tensor Cores in CUTLASS](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/on-demand/session/gtcspring21-s31883/)
242	- [Accelerating Backward Data Gradient by Increasing Tensor Core Utilization in CUTLASS](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/on-demand/session/gtcspring22-s41996/)
243	- [CUTLASS: Python API, Enhancements, and NVIDIA Hopper](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/on-demand/session/gtcfall22-a41131/)
244	
245	# Building CUTLASS
246	
247	CUTLASS is a header-only template library and does not need to be built to be used by other
248	projects. Client applications should target CUTLASS's `include/` directory in their include
249	paths.
250	
251	CUTLASS unit tests, examples, and utilities can be build with CMake.
252	The minimum version of CMake is given in the [Quickstart guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/quickstart.html).
253	Make sure the `CUDACXX` environment  variable points to NVCC in the CUDA Toolkit installed
254	on your system.
255	
256	```bash
257	$ export CUDACXX=${CUDA_INSTALL_PATH}/bin/nvcc
258	```
259	
260	Create a build directory within the CUTLASS project, then run CMake. By default CUTLASS will build kernels
261	for CUDA architecture versions 5.0, 6.0, 6.1, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.6, 8.9, and 9.0.
262	To reduce compile time you can specify
263	the architectures to build CUTLASS for by changing the CMake configuration setting
264	`CUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS`.
265	
266	```bash
267	$ mkdir build && cd build
268	
269	$ cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS=80               # compiles for NVIDIA's Ampere Architecture
270	```
271	
272	From the `build/` directory, compile and run the CUTLASS unit tests by building the target `test_unit` with make.
273	
274	The unit tests are organized as several binaries mirroring the top-level namespaces of CUTLASS,
275	and they may be executed in parallel via make's `-j` command line argument.
276	
277	```bash
278	$ make test_unit -j
279	...
280	...
281	...
282	[----------] Global test environment tear-down
283	[==========] 946 tests from 57 test cases ran. (10812 ms total)
284	[  PASSED  ] 946 tests.
285	```
286	
287	All tests should pass on supported platforms, though the exact number of tests may vary over time.
288	
289	
290	# Project Structure
291	
292	CUTLASS is arranged as a header-only library along with Utilities, Tools, Examples, and unit tests.
293	[Doxygen documentation](https://nvidia.github.io/cutlass) provides a complete list of files, classes,
294	and template concepts defined in the CUTLASS project.
295	
296	A detailed explanation of the source code organization may be found in the
297	[CUTLASS documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/code_organization.html), but several main components are summarized below.
298	
299	## CUTLASS Template Library
300	
301	```
302	include/                     # client applications should target this directory in their build's include paths
303	
304	  cutlass/                   # CUDA Templates for Linear Algebra Subroutines and Solvers - headers only
305	
306	    arch/                    # direct exposure of architecture features (including instruction-level GEMMs)
307	
308	    conv/                    # code specialized for convolution
309	
310	    epilogue/                # code specialized for the epilogue of gemm/convolution
311	
312	    gemm/                    # code specialized for general matrix product computations
313	
314	    layout/                  # layout definitions for matrices, tensors, and other mathematical objects in memory
315	
316	    platform/                # CUDA-capable Standard Library components
317	
318	    reduction/               # bandwidth-limited reduction kernels that do not fit the "gemm" model
319	
320	    thread/                  # simt code that can be performed within a CUDA thread
321	
322	    transform/               # code specialized for layout, type, and domain transformations
323	
324	    *                        # core vocabulary types, containers, and basic numeric operations
325	
326	  cute/                      # CuTe Layout, layout algebra, MMA/Copy atoms, tiled MMA/Copy
327	
328	    algorithm/               # Definitions of core operations such as copy, gemm, and operations on cute::tuples
329	
330	    arch/                    # Bare bones PTX wrapper structs for copy and math instructions
331	
332	    atom/                    # Meta-information either link to or built from arch/ operators
333	
334	      mma_atom.hpp           # cute::Mma_Atom and cute::TiledMma
335	
336	      copy_atom.hpp          # cute::Copy_Atom and cute::TiledCopy
337	
338	      *sm*.hpp               # Arch specific meta-information for copy and math operations
339	
340	    *                        # Core library types such as Shape, Stride, Layout, Tensor, and associated operations
341	
342	```
343	
344	### CUTLASS SDK Examples
345	
346	[CUTLASS SDK examples](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/tree/main/examples) apply CUTLASS templates to implement basic computations.
347	
348	### Tools
349	
350	```
351	tools/
352	  library/                   # CUTLASS Instance Library - contains instantiations of all supported CUTLASS templates
353	    include/
354	      cutlass/
355	        library/
356	
357	  profiler/                  # CUTLASS Profiler         - command-line utility for executing operations in the
358	                             #                            CUTLASS Library
359	
360	  util/                      # CUTLASS Utilities        - contains numerous helper classes for
361	    include/                 #                            managing tensors in device memory, reference
362	      cutlass/               #                            implementations for GEMM, random initialization
363	        util/                #                            of tensors, and I/O.
364	```
365	
366	### Test
367	
368	The `test/unit/` directory consist of unit tests implemented with Google Test that demonstrate
369	basic usage of Core API components and complete tests of the CUTLASS GEMM computations.
370	
371	Instructions for building and running the Unit tests are described in the [Quickstart guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/quickstart.html).
372	
373	# Performance Profiling

![ALT](./media/images/gemm-hierarchy-with-epilogue-no-labels.png "Complete CUDA GEMM decomposition")
# Overview

# CUTLASS 4.6.0

_CUTLASS 4.6.0 - June 2026_

CUTLASS is a collection of abstractions for implementing high-performance matrix-matrix multiplication (GEMM)
and related computations at all levels and scales within CUDA. It incorporates strategies for
hierarchical decomposition and data movement. CUTLASS decomposes these "moving parts" into reusable, modular
software components and abstractions.

Primitives for different levels of a conceptual parallelization hierarchy can be specialized and tuned
via custom tiling sizes, data types, and other algorithmic policy. The resulting flexibility simplifies
their use as building blocks within custom kernels and applications.

CUTLASS has been providing CUDA C++ template abstractions for high-performance linear algebra since 2017 and
these abstractions provide extensive support for a wide range of computations including
mixed-precision computations, specialized data-movement (async copy) and
multiply-accumulate abstractions for FP64, FP32, TF32, FP16, BF16,
[FP32 emulation via tensor core instruction](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/tree/main/examples/27_ampere_3xtf32_fast_accurate_tensorop_gemm),
 8b floating point types (e5m2 and e4m3),
 block scaled data types (NVIDIA NVFP4 and OCP standard MXFP4, MXFP6, MXFP8),
 narrow integer types (4 and 8b signed and unsigned integers),
 and binary 1b data types (where architectures allow for the
native support of such data types) across NVIDIA's Volta, Turing, Ampere, Ada, Hopper, and Blackwell architectures.

To this rich ecosystem of C++ based kernel programming abstractions, CUTLASS 4 adds CUTLASS DSLs. These are Python native interfaces for writing high-performance CUDA kernels based on core CUTLASS and CuTe concepts without any performance compromises. This allows for a much smoother learning curve, orders of magnitude faster compile times, native integration with DL frameworks without writing glue code, and much more intuitive metaprogramming that does not require deep C++ expertise.

Overall we envision CUTLASS DSLs as a family of domain-specific languages (DSLs). With the release of 4.0, we are releasing the first of these in CuTe DSL. This is a low level programming model that is fully consistent with CuTe C++ abstractions — exposing core concepts such as layouts, tensors, hardware atoms, and full control over the hardware thread and data hierarchy.

CuTe DSL demonstrates optimal matrix multiply and other linear algebra operations
targeting the programmable, high-throughput _Tensor Cores_ implemented by
NVIDIA's Ampere, Hopper, and Blackwell architectures.

We believe it will become an indispensable tool for students, researchers, and performance
engineers alike — flattening the learning curve of GPU programming, rapidly prototyping kernel
designs, and bringing optimized solutions into production.

CuTe DSL is currently in public beta and will graduate out of beta by end of summer 2026.

To get started quickly - please refer :
  - [CUTLASS C++ Quick Start Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/quickstart.html).
  - [CuTe DSL Quick Start Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/pythonDSL/quick_start.html).

# What's New in CUTLASS 4.6

## CuTe DSL
* New features
  - Supported AoT cross-compilation for aarch64?~@~Qlinux?~@~Qgnu
  - Support for two launch attributes: launch completion events (cudaLaunchAttributeLaunchCompletionEvent), for recording an event once all thread blocks have begun executing, and launch programatic events (cudaLaunchAttributeProgrammaticEvent), for PDL event-based synchronization
  - Supported auto calculating per-kernel shared memory carveout preference, or use new laucnch option `preferred_smem_carveout` to set manually.
  - Auto-deduced smem size for launching kernels
    - Launch config `smem` now defaults to `None` for auto-calculating kernel shared memory usage, which is recommended unless manual control is required.
    - Warnings will be raised when the manually set shared memory size is insufficient or exceeds the GPU maximum.
    - The default shared memory usage calculation aligns with CUDA C++ static shared memory behavior, i.e. summing all allocations additively.
    - An additional launch option `smem_merge_branch_allocs` is provided to merge shared memory allocations across mutually exclusive code branches, which is recommended for inlined mega-kernels to reduce total footprint.

* Bug fixing and improvements
  - Improvements on linter support with more type ignores cleaned up
  - Improvements on tvm-ffi CUDA runtime error diagnostics
  - Improvements on dataclass support for TVM-FFI
  - Fixed a regression on compilation time
  - Enhancement on compile time checks to reject mis-aligned smem operand for TMA
  - Long-deprecated API clean-up, including:
    - cute.core.ThrMma, please use cute.ThrMma instead
    - cute.core.ThrCopy, please use cute.ThrCopy instead
    - cute.make_fragment, please use cute.make_rmem_tensor instead

## CUTLASS C++
* Add [example 113](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/tree/main/examples/113_hopper_gemm_activation_fusion) for Hopper GEMM with activation fusion.
  - Supports standard and gated activations (e.g., SiLu) with fp8 and fp16 inputs.
  - Covers both regular GEMM and grouped GEMM variants.
* Improve SM90 grouped/ptr-array GEMM with EVT support.
  - Adds the EVT (Epilogue Visitor Tree) plumbing required to do activation, bias, and auxiliary-tensor fusion inside SM90 grouped and ptr-array GEMM kernels.
* Fix `DescriptorIterator::operator+` in `mma_traits_sm100.hpp` to use 32-bit arithmetic on CUDA toolkit version <= 13.3, preserving the high half of the smem descriptor.

Note: CUTLASS 4.x builds are known to be down on Windows platforms for all CUDA toolkits.
CUTLASS team is working on a fix.

**See the [CHANGELOG](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/CHANGELOG.html) for details of all past releases and updates.**

# Performance

CUTLASS primitives are very efficient.  When used to construct device-wide GEMM kernels,
they exhibit nearly optimal utilization of peak theoretical throughput. The figure below
shows CUTLASS 3.8's performance as a % of theoretical peak utilization
on various input and output data types when run on NVIDIA Blackwell SM100 architecture GPU.

![ALT](media/images/cutlass-3.8-blackwell-gemm-peak-performance.svg "")

The two figures below show the continual CUTLASS performance improvements
on an [NVIDIA H100](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/h100/) (NVIDIA Hopper architecture) since
CUTLASS 3.1.
CUTLASS 3.5.1 was compiled with the [CUDA 12.5u1 Toolkit](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads).
Tensor Core operations are implemented using CUDA's
[mma](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/index.html#warp-level-matrix-instructions-mma) and
[wgmma](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/index.html#asynchronous-warpgroup-level-matrix-instructions) instructions.

![ALT](media/images/cutlass-3.5.1-gemm-peak-performance.png "")
![ALT](media/images/cutlass-3.5.1-gemm-peak-performance-fp8.png "")

# CuTe

CUTLASS 3.0 introduced a new core library, CuTe, to describe and manipulate tensors of threads and data.
CuTe is a collection of C++ CUDA template abstractions for
defining and operating on hierarchically multidimensional layouts of threads and data.
CuTe provides `Layout` and `Tensor` objects that compactly package the type,
shape, memory space, and layout of data, while performing the complicated indexing for the user.
This lets programmers focus on the logical descriptions of their algorithms while
CuTe does the mechanical bookkeeping for them. With these tools, we can quickly design,
implement, and modify all dense linear algebra operations.

The core abstractions of CuTe are hierarchically multidimensional layouts
which can be composed with data arrays to represent tensors.
The representation of layouts is powerful enough to represent nearly
everything we need to implement efficient dense linear algebra.
Layouts can also be combined and manipulated via functional composition, on which we build a large set of common operations such as tiling and partitioning.

CUTLASS 3.0 and beyond adopts CuTe throughout the GEMM hierarchy in its templates.
This greatly simplifies the design and improves code composability and readability.
More documentation specific to CuTe can be found in its
[dedicated documentation directory](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/cute/00_quickstart.html).

# Compatibility

Minimum requirements:

- Architecture: Volta (compute capability 7.0)
- Compiler: Must support at least C++17
- CUDA Toolkit version: 11.4

CUTLASS requires a C++17 host compiler and
performs best when built with the [**CUDA 12.8 Toolkit**](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads).
It is also compatible with CUDA 11.4, CUDA 11.5, CUDA 11.6, CUDA 11.7, CUDA 11.8, and all other CUDA 12.x versions.

## Operating Systems

We have tested the following environments.

|**Operating System** | **Compiler** |
|-----------------|----------|
| Ubuntu 18.04 | GCC 7.5.0  |
| Ubuntu 20.04 | GCC 10.3.0 |
| Ubuntu 22.04 | GCC 11.2.0 |

Note: GCC 8.5.0 has known regressions regarding fold expressions and overloaded operators. Using GCC 7.5.0 or (preferred) GCC >= 9 is recommended.

Note: CUTLASS 3.x builds are known to be down on Windows platforms for all CUDA toolkits.
CUTLASS team is working on a fix.

## Hardware

## Target Architecture

In general, PTX code generated for one target architecture can be run on future architectures
(i.e., it is forward compatible).
However, CUDA 12.0 introduced the concept of "architecture-accelerated features" whose
PTX does not have forward compatibility guarantees.
Several Hopper and Blackwell PTX instructions fall under this category of
architecture-accelerated features, and thus require a `sm_90a` or `sm100a` target architecture
(note the "a" appended). For more details on this and other architecture-accelerated instructions,
please refer to the [CUDA Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-c-programming-guide/index.html#feature-availability).

The target architecture information is passed on to CUTLASS via the cmake flag
`CUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS`. In order to maximize performance on Hopper GH100,
users are required to build CUTLASS with `90a` as the target architecture.
If a user accidentally builds a kernel which uses SM90a features
(e.g. Hopper Tensor Core Instructions), using the SM90 target
(note the lack of "a"), with either CUDA Toolkit 12 or 11.8,
the kernel is expected to fail with a runtime error.

```
cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS="90a"
```
Or

```
cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS="100a"
```

Note: The NVIDIA Blackwell SM100 architecture used in the datacenter
products has a different compute capability than the one underpinning
NVIDIA Blackwell GeForce RTX 50 series GPUs (SM120). As a result, kernels
compiled for Blackwell SM100 architecture with arch conditional features
(using `sm100a`) are not compatible with RTX 50 series GPUs.

Please refer to the [functionality documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/functionality.html)
for details on which kernels require which target architectures.

# Building CUTLASS

CUTLASS is a header-only template library and does not need to be built to be used by other
projects. Client applications should target CUTLASS's `include/` directory in their include
paths.

CUTLASS unit tests, examples, and utilities can be build with CMake.
The minimum version of CMake is given in the [Quickstart guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/quickstart.html).
Make sure the `CUDACXX` environment  variable points to NVCC in the CUDA Toolkit installed
on your system.

```bash
$ export CUDACXX=${CUDA_INSTALL_PATH}/bin/nvcc
```

Create a build directory within the CUTLASS project, then run CMake. By default CUTLASS will build kernels
for CUDA architecture versions 5.0, 6.0, 6.1, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.6, 8.9, and 9.0.
To reduce compile time you can specify
the architectures to build CUTLASS for by changing the CMake configuration setting
`CUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS`.

```bash
$ mkdir build && cd build

$ cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS=80               # compiles for NVIDIA's Ampere Architecture
```

From the `build/` directory, compile and run the CUTLASS unit tests by building the target `test_unit` with make.

The unit tests are organized as several binaries mirroring the top-level namespaces of CUTLASS,
and they may be executed in parallel via make's `-j` command line argument.

```bash
$ make test_unit -j
...
...
...
[----------] Global test environment tear-down
[==========] 946 tests from 57 test cases ran. (10812 ms total)
[  PASSED  ] 946 tests.
```

All tests should pass on supported platforms, though the exact number of tests may vary over time.


# Project Structure

CUTLASS is arranged as a header-only library along with Utilities, Tools, Examples, and unit tests.
[Doxygen documentation](https://nvidia.github.io/cutlass) provides a complete list of files, classes,
and template concepts defined in the CUTLASS project.

A detailed explanation of the source code organization may be found in the
[CUTLASS documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/code_organization.html), but several main components are summarized below.

## CUTLASS Template Library

```
include/                     # client applications should target this directory in their build's include paths

  cutlass/                   # CUDA Templates for Linear Algebra Subroutines and Solvers - headers only

    arch/                    # direct exposure of architecture features (including instruction-level GEMMs)

    conv/                    # code specialized for convolution

    epilogue/                # code specialized for the epilogue of gemm/convolution

    gemm/                    # code specialized for general matrix product computations

    layout/                  # layout definitions for matrices, tensors, and other mathematical objects in memory

    platform/                # CUDA-capable Standard Library components

    reduction/               # bandwidth-limited reduction kernels that do not fit the "gemm" model

    thread/                  # simt code that can be performed within a CUDA thread

    transform/               # code specialized for layout, type, and domain transformations

    *                        # core vocabulary types, containers, and basic numeric operations

  cute/                      # CuTe Layout, layout algebra, MMA/Copy atoms, tiled MMA/Copy

    algorithm/               # Definitions of core operations such as copy, gemm, and operations on cute::tuples

    arch/                    # Bare bones PTX wrapper structs for copy and math instructions

    atom/                    # Meta-information either link to or built from arch/ operators

      mma_atom.hpp           # cute::Mma_Atom and cute::TiledMma

      copy_atom.hpp          # cute::Copy_Atom and cute::TiledCopy

      *sm*.hpp               # Arch specific meta-information for copy and math operations

    *                        # Core library types such as Shape, Stride, Layout, Tensor, and associated operations

```

### CUTLASS SDK Examples

[CUTLASS SDK examples](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/tree/main/examples) apply CUTLASS templates to implement basic computations.

### Tools

```
tools/
  library/                   # CUTLASS Instance Library - contains instantiations of all supported CUTLASS templates
    include/
      cutlass/
        library/

  profiler/                  # CUTLASS Profiler         - command-line utility for executing operations in the
                             #                            CUTLASS Library

  util/                      # CUTLASS Utilities        - contains numerous helper classes for
    include/                 #                            managing tensors in device memory, reference
      cutlass/               #                            implementations for GEMM, random initialization
        util/                #                            of tensors, and I/O.
```

### Test

The `test/unit/` directory consist of unit tests implemented with Google Test that demonstrate
basic usage of Core API components and complete tests of the CUTLASS GEMM computations.

Instructions for building and running the Unit tests are described in the [Quickstart guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/quickstart.html).

# Performance Profiling

The `tools/profiler/` directory contains a command-line utility for launching each of the GEMM kernels.
It can be built as follows:

```bash
$ make cutlass_profiler -j16
```
## Building all GEMM and Convolution kernels (_long_ build times)

By default, only one tile size is instantiated for each data type, math instruction, and layout.
To instantiate all, set the following environment variable when running CMake from an empty `build/` directory.
Beware, this results in *tens of thousands* of kernels and long build times.
This would also result in a large binary size and on some platforms linker to fail on building the library.
Therefore, it's highly recommended to generate only a subset of kernels as demonstrated in the sub-section below.
```bash
$ cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS=90a -DCUTLASS_LIBRARY_KERNELS=all
...
$ make cutlass_profiler -j16
```

## Building a subset of GEMM and Convolution kernels (_reduced_ build times)

To compile strictly one kernel or a small set of kernels, a comma-delimited list of kernel names with
wildcard characters may be used to reduce the set of kernels. The following examples show building exactly one
or a subset of kernels for NVIDIA Ampere and Turing architecture:

### Building a subset Tensor Core GEMM kernels

To compile a subset of Tensor Core GEMM kernels with FP32 accumulation and FP16 input targeting NVIDIA Ampere and Turing architecture,
use the below cmake command line:
```bash
$ cmake .. -DCUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS='75;80' -DCUTLASS_LIBRARY_KERNELS=cutlass_tensorop_s*gemm_f16_*_nt_align8
...
$ make cutlass_profiler -j16
```

I now have a thorough understanding of the CUTLASS project. Let me consolidate my understanding and design the learning handbook.

## Project Understanding

**CUTLASS** (CUDA Templates for Linear Algebra) is NVIDIA's header-only C++ template library for high-performance GEMM (matrix multiplication) and related computations on NVIDIA GPUs. Version 4.6.0. It's a massive project (~7000 files).

Key components:
1. **CUTLASS C++ core library** (`include/cutlass/`) - the 2.x/3.x template library
   - `arch/` - direct exposure of architecture features (instruction-level GEMMs, MMA)
   - `gemm/` - GEMM computations (device, kernel, collective, threadblock, warp, thread)
   - `conv/` - convolution (implicit GEMM)
   - `epilogue/` - epilogue code (including EVT - Epilogue Visitor Tree)
   - `layout/` - matrix/tensor layouts
   - `numeric_types` - half, bfloat16, float8, tfloat32, etc.
   - `pipeline/` - async pipeline (Hopper mainloop)

2. **CuTe** (`include/cute/`) - the modern core (3.0+) for layouts and tensors
   - `layout.hpp`, `tensor.hpp`, `int_tuple.hpp`, `stride.hpp` - core types
   - `algorithm/` - copy, gemm operations
   - `atom/` - MMA/Copy atoms (mma_atom, copy_atom)
   - `arch/` - PTX wrappers
   - `container/` - tuple, array

3. **CuTe DSL** (Python) (`python/CuTeDSL/`, `python/pycute/`) - Python native interface (CUTLASS 4.x)

4. **Tools**
   - `tools/library/` - Instance Library (auto-generated kernel instantiations)
   - `tools/profiler/` - cutlass_profiler CLI
   - `tools/util/` - helper classes

5. **Examples** (~95 examples) - from 00_basic_gemm to 95_blackwell_*

6. **CuTe C++ tutorial** (`examples/cute/tutorial/`) - sgemm_1.cu, sgemm_2.cu, sgemm_sm70.cu, sgemm_sm80.cu, tiled_copy.cu

7. **Python DSL tutorials** (`examples/python/CuTeDSL/dsl_tutorials/`, `cute/notebooks/`)

The architecture is hierarchical: device → cluster → threadblock (CTA) → warp → thread, with hierarchical tiling.

The GEMM hierarchy:
- Device kernel (`gemm/device/`)
- Kernel (`gemm/kernel/`) - gemm_universal
- Collective MMA (`gemm/collective/`) - the mainloop, e.g. sm90_gemm_tma_warpspecialized, sm100_mma_warpspecialized
- Epilogue (`epilogue/`)
- Tile scheduler

Key API eras:
- 2.x API: `cutlass::gemm::device::Gemm` (00_basic_gemm)
- 3.x API: CuTe-based, `GemmUniversal`, CollectiveBuilder, kernel dispatch policies (49_hopper_gemm_with_collective_builder)
- 4.x: CuTe DSL (Python)

input.rationale: CUTLASS 是 NVIDIA 的高性能 CUDA 矩阵乘法（GEMM）模板库，规模庞大且包含三代 API：2.x（device::Gemm）、3.x（基于 CuTe 的 GemmUniversal/CollectiveBuilder）与 4.x（Python CuTe DSL）。学习路线因此按“先认识项目与经典 2.x API → 再掌握 CuTe 核心抽象与 3.x 通用模型 → 最后进入高级架构与 DSL”三层推进。入门层先让读者建立项目定位、构建运行、目录入口与数值/布局等基本概念，并用最简单的 00_basic_gemm 跑通第一个 GEMM。进阶层聚焦 CUTLASS 3.x 的灵魂——CuTe 的 Layout/Tensor/Atom 抽象，以及 GEMM 的 kernel/collective/epilogue 三段式通用模型与 CollectiveBuilder，配合 Hopper 的 warp-specialized 内核落地。专家层覆盖异步流水线、TMA、Stream-K 调度、Grouped/卷积/低精度/Blackwell 等高级机制，以及 profiler 实例库和 Python CuTe DSL 两条产出路径。讲义之间通过 depends_on 形成清晰依赖链，确保读者始终能由浅入深、结合真实源码与可操作实践逐步深入。

lectures.topic: 介绍 CUTLASS 是什么、解决什么问题、它在 CUDA 生态中的定位，以及三代 API（2.x/3.x/4.x DSL）的演进。
learning_goals: 理解 CUTLASS 作为 header-only CUDA 模板库的定位与价值
了解 GEMM 层次化分解的核心思想

支持的架构与数据类型

lectures.title: CuTe Layout 与布局代数
lectures.topic: 讲解 CuTe 的 Layout 抽象：Shape、Stride、层级化多维布局，以及布局的函数式组合与分块（tiling）。

掌握层级化 int_tuple 表示
学会用 composition/tiling 操作布局

lectures.title: CuTe Tensor 与引擎

lectures.title: CuTe 算法：copy 与 gemm
lectures.topic: 讲解 cute::copy、cute::gemm 等核心算法，它们如何作用于 CuTe Tensor 完成数据搬运与乘加。

lectures.title: CUTLASS arch：指令级 MMA
lectures.topic: 讲解 cutlass/arch 与 cute/arch 中的 MMA 指令封装，从 Volta 到 Hopper/Blackwell 的 mma 指令演进。

lectures.title: 低精度与 Block-Scaled 类型

learning_goals: 理解 SM100 UMMA 指令与 tensor memory（TMEM）

了解 cluster 与分布式 GEMM 的扩展
minimal_modules: 分布式 GEMM 扩展点
lectures.practice_task: 对比 SM90 与 SM100 collective 的 producer/consumer 结构，指出 TMEM 在 SM100 中取代了哪个角色。

lectures.title: CUTLASS Profiler 与实例库
lectures.topic: 讲解 cutlass_profiler 命令行工具与 tools/library 实例库如何自动生成与调优大量内核。
learning_goals: 掌握 cutlass_profiler 的使用与过滤选项
理解 CUTLASS_LIBRARY_KERNELS 的作用
了解实例库的代码生成机制
minimal_modules: 选择性编译以减少耗时
lectures.practice_task: 用 CUTLASS_LIBRARY_KERNELS 过滤只编译一类 FP16 GEMM 内核，并用 cutlass_profiler 测量其 GFLOP/s。

lectures.title: CuTe DSL（Python）：布局与张量
lectures.topic: 讲解 Python 版 CuTe DSL 的 Layout/Tensor 抽象，以及如何用 Python 表达与 C++ CuTe 一致的概念。
learning_goals: 理解 CuTe DSL 的 Python Layout/Tensor API
掌握 DSL 与 C++ CuTe 概念的一致性
了解 DSL 的编译与运行模型
lectures.practice_task: 运行 hello_world.ipynb，并用 Python DSL 构造一个 (4,4):(1,4) 的 Layout，验证其索引映射与 C++ 一致。

lectures.title: CuTe DSL（Python）：GEMM 实践
lectures.topic: 讲解如何用 Python CuTe DSL 编写一个完整的 GEMM 内核，结合 atom、TiledCopy 与 launch。
learning_goals: 掌握用 DSL 组装 GEMM 主循环的步骤
理解 DSL 中的 atom 与 TiledMma 用法
了解 DSL 内核的编译、launch 与 autotune
lectures.practice_task: 阅读 dense_gemm.py，标注其 mainloop 中的拷贝与乘加步骤，并尝试修改 tile shape 重新运行对比性能。

data.head: e8ecfad75b44d1ad56264f5001d877e9e47fe080
data.rationale: CUTLASS 是 NVIDIA 的高性能 CUDA 矩阵乘法（GEMM）模板库，规模庞大且包含三代 API：2.x（device::Gemm）、3.x（基于 CuTe 的 GemmUniversal/CollectiveBuilder）与 4.x（Python CuTe DSL）。学习路线因此按“先认识项目与经典 2.x API → 再掌握 CuTe 核心抽象与 3.x 通用模型 → 最后进入高级架构与 DSL”三层推进。入门层先让读者建立项目定位、构建运行、目录入口与数值/布局等基本概念，并用最简单的 00_basic_gemm 跑通第一个 GEMM。进阶层聚焦 CUTLASS 3.x 的灵魂——CuTe 的 Layout/Tensor/Atom 抽象，以及 GEMM 的 kernel/collective/epilogue 三段式通用模型与 CollectiveBuilder，配合 Hopper 的 warp-specialized 内核落地。专家层覆盖异步流水线、TMA、Stream-K 调度、Grouped/卷积/低精度/Blackwell 等高级机制，以及 profiler 实例库和 Python CuTe DSL 两条产出路径。讲义之间通过 depends_on 形成清晰依赖链，确保读者始终能由浅入深、结合真实源码与可操作实践逐步深入。

lectures.title: CUTLASS 项目总览与定位
lectures.topic: 介绍 CUTLASS 是什么、解决什么问题、它在 CUDA 生态中的定位，以及三代 API（2.x/3.x/4.x DSL）的演进。
learning_goals: 理解 CUTLASS 作为 header-only CUDA 模板库的定位与价值
了解 GEMM 层次化分解的核心思想
区分 CUTLASS 2.x、3.x（CuTe）与 4.x（CuTe DSL）三代 API
minimal_modules: CUTLASS 是什么与适用场景
三代 API 演进时间线
支持的架构与数据类型
lectures.practice_task: 阅读 README.md，列出 CUTLASS 支持的 GPU 架构（compute capability）与至少 4 种数据类型，并用自己的话写一段说明 CUTLASS 与 cuBLAS 的关系。

lectures.title: 构建与运行 CUTLASS
lectures.topic: 讲解如何用 CMake 构建 CUTLASS，设置 CUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS 目标架构，编译并运行单元测试与示例。
learning_goals: 掌握 CUTLASS 的 CMake 构建流程与关键变量
理解 CUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS 与架构加速特性（如 90a/100a）
学会编译运行单元测试 test_unit 与单个示例
lectures.practice_task: 创建 build 目录，用 cmake 指定 CUTLASS_NVCC_ARCHS=80 构建项目，编译并运行 examples/00_basic_gemm，记录输出结果。

lectures.title: 目录结构与源码组织
lectures.topic: 梳理 CUTLASS 仓库的目录结构：include（cutlass 与 cute 两套核心）、tools、examples、test 的职责划分。
learning_goals: 掌握 include/cutlass 各子目录（gemm/conv/epilogue/layout/arch）的职责
区分 cutlass 与 cute 两套核心库
了解 tools（library/profiler/util）与 examples/test 的作用
lectures.practice_task: 在仓库中分别定位 GEMM、卷积、epilogue、layout 对应的头文件目录，画一张目录职责思维导图。

lectures.title: 数值类型与基础容器
lectures.topic: 讲解 CUTLASS 如何用 C++ 类型表达半精度、bf16、fp8、tf32 等数据类型，以及 Array 容器与数值转换。
learning_goals: 认识 cutlass::half_t、bfloat16_t、float8_e4m3_t 等类型
理解 cutlass::Array 容器及其对齐特性
了解 numeric_conversion 的作用
minimal_modules: 半精度与 bf16 类型
lectures.practice_task: 编写一个小程序，定义一个 cutlass::Array<half_t, 4>，用 numeric_conversion 将 float 数组转换成 half_t 并打印。

lectures.title: 矩阵布局基础
lectures.topic: 讲解 CUTLASS 2.x 的 layout 抽象：RowMajor/ColumnMajor、TensorRef/TensorView 以及 pitch-linear 概念。
learning_goals: 理解 RowMajor/ColumnMajor 等 layout tag 的作用
掌握 TensorRef/TensorView 的用法
了解 pitch-linear 与 leading dimension 的概念
lectures.practice_task: 用 cutlass::layout::ColumnMajor 构造一个 TensorRef 指向一个 4x4 列主序矩阵，并用 TensorView 访问并打印元素。

lectures.title: 第一个 GEMM：2.x device API
lectures.topic: 通过 examples/00_basic_gemm 讲解如何用 cutlass::gemm::device::Gemm 模板实例化并启动一个 SGEMM 内核。
learning_goals: 掌握 cutlass::gemm::device::Gemm 模板参数的含义
理解 Gemm args 对象与 argument 传递模式
学会调用 gemm_operator 并验证结果
minimal_modules: Gemm args 与 argument 对象
lectures.practice_task: 修改 examples/00_basic_gemm，将矩阵改为行主序（RowMajor），重新编译运行并与参考实现对比结果。

learning_goals: 掌握用 CollectiveBuilder 构建完整 Hopper GEMM 的步骤
理解 warp specialization 在 Hopper 的体现
了解 epilogue 与 launch 的衔接
lectures.practice_task: 基于 example 49，将其改为 BF16 输入、FP32 累加的 Hopper GEMM，编译并对照参考实现验证。

lectures.title: Epilogue 与 EVT 访客树
lectures.topic: 讲解 CUTLASS 的 epilogue 机制，重点介绍 EVT（Epilogue Visitor Tree）如何灵活组合激活、偏置、缩放等后处理。
learning_goals: 理解 epilogue collective 的结构
掌握 EVT 访客树的组合模型
了解 fusion 目录下 visitor/callback 的作用
minimal_modules: 常用 epilogue 融合模式
lectures.practice_task: 在 EVT 文档/源码中找到一个 LinearCombination + ReLU 的组合，画出对应的访客树结构并说明数据流向。

units.title: 专家：高级架构机制与 CuTe DSL

lectures.title: Async Pipeline 与 Warp Specialization
lectures.topic: 讲解 Hopper 的异步流水线（cutlass/pipeline）与 warp specialization，如何用 producer/consumer 重叠拷贝与计算。
learning_goals: 理解异步流水线的 stage 与缓冲管理
掌握 warp specialization 的 producer/consumer 分工
了解 sm90_pipeline 的同步原语
lectures.practice_task: 分析 sm90_mma_tma_warpspecialized 的主循环，标注出 producer warp 与 consumer warp 各自执行的 stage 数与等待点。

lectures.title: TMA 异步张量拷贝
lectures.topic: 讲解 Hopper/Blackwell 的 TMA（Tensor Memory Accelerator）拷贝机制，描述符构造与 TiledCopy。
learning_goals: 理解 TMA 描述符（descriptor）的构造
掌握 cute/arch 中 TMA 拷贝的封装
了解 TMA 与 swizzled smem 的配合
lectures.practice_task: 找到 TMA 描述符的构造代码，写一段说明其如何表达一个多维张量盒（box）与步长，并指出对齐要求。

lectures.title: Tile Scheduling 与 Stream-K
lectures.topic: 讲解 tile scheduler 如何把 GEMM 的输出 tile 分配给 CTA/cluster，以及 Stream-K 模式如何提升尾部利用率。
learning_goals: 理解 tile scheduler 的调度策略
掌握 Stream-K 的分块与跨 CTA 协作
了解持久化（persistent）内核与 scheduler 的关系
lectures.practice_task: 对比普通 scheduler 与 Stream-K scheduler 的参数，说明 Stream-K 在哪些问题形状下收益最大。

lectures.title: Grouped 与 Batched GEMM
lectures.topic: 讲解一次性处理多个不同形状矩阵乘的 Grouped GEMM 与相同形状的 Batched GEMM 机制与示例。
learning_goals: 理解 Grouped GEMM 的 problem shape 数组模型
掌握 Batched GEMM 的 batch 维处理
了解 grouped GEMM 的调度与内存布局
lectures.practice_task: 阅读 example 24，列出 Grouped GEMM 的 arguments 需要提供哪些数组（ptr_A/stride 等），并解释其调度方式。

lectures.title: 隐式 GEMM 卷积
lectures.topic: 讲解 CUTLASS 如何把卷积转化为隐式 GEMM，包括 NHWC 布局、problem shape 与 collective conv。
learning_goals: 理解隐式 GEMM 的 im2col 思想
掌握 conv 的 problem shape（N,H,W,C,K 等）
了解 conv collective 与 GEMM collective 的复用关系
lectures.practice_task: 给定一个 conv2d 的 N/H/W/C/K/R/S，计算其等价 GEMM 的 (M,N,K)，并说明哪些维度来自 im2col。

lectures.title: 低精度与 Block-Scaled 类型
lectures.topic: 讲解 FP8、NVFP4/MXFP 等 block-scaled 数据类型及其在 GEMM 中的缩放因子处理。
learning_goals: 理解 FP8/NVFP4 等窄精度类型的表示
掌握 block scaling（逐块缩放因子）机制
了解 narrow precision GEMM 的 collective 选择
minimal_modules: 缩放因子加载与反量化
lectures.practice_task: 阅读 example 72，说明 NVFP4 输入时缩放因子（scale tensor）的形状如何与 GEMM 分块对齐。

lectures.title: Blackwell SM100 集体 GEMM
lectures.topic: 讲解 Blackwell SM100 的 GEMM 集体实现，包括 UMMA 指令、TMEM、cluster 与分布式共享内存。
learning_goals: 理解 SM100 UMMA 指令与 tensor memory（TMEM）
掌握 Blackwell collective MMA 的结构
了解 cluster 与分布式 GEMM 的扩展
minimal_modules: 分布式 GEMM 扩展点
lectures.practice_task: 对比 SM90 与 SM100 collective 的 producer/consumer 结构，指出 TMEM 在 SM100 中取代了哪个角色。

lectures.title: CUTLASS Profiler 与实例库
lectures.topic: 讲解 cutlass_profiler 命令行工具与 tools/library 实例库如何自动生成与调优大量内核。
learning_goals: 掌握 cutlass_profiler 的使用与过滤选项
理解 CUTLASS_LIBRARY_KERNELS 的作用
了解实例库的代码生成机制
minimal_modules: 选择性编译以减少耗时
lectures.practice_task: 用 CUTLASS_LIBRARY_KERNELS 过滤只编译一类 FP16 GEMM 内核，并用 cutlass_profiler 测量其 GFLOP/s。

lectures.title: CuTe DSL（Python）：布局与张量
lectures.topic: 讲解 Python 版 CuTe DSL 的 Layout/Tensor 抽象，以及如何用 Python 表达与 C++ CuTe 一致的概念。
learning_goals: 理解 CuTe DSL 的 Python Layout/Tensor API
掌握 DSL 与 C++ CuTe 概念的一致性
了解 DSL 的编译与运行模型
lectures.practice_task: 运行 hello_world.ipynb，并用 Python DSL 构造一个 (4,4):(1,4) 的 Layout，验证其索引映射与 C++ 一致。

lectures.title: CuTe DSL（Python）：GEMM 实践
lectures.topic: 讲解如何用 Python CuTe DSL 编写一个完整的 GEMM 内核，结合 atom、TiledCopy 与 launch。
learning_goals: 掌握用 DSL 组装 GEMM 主循环的步骤
理解 DSL 中的 atom 与 TiledMma 用法
了解 DSL 内核的编译、launch 与 autotune
lectures.practice_task: 阅读 dense_gemm.py，标注其 mainloop 中的拷贝与乘加步骤，并尝试修改 tile shape 重新运行对比性能。
